It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 19 of about 42 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Interesting! Clicking (34, 17), which is to the right of the magenta square at (34, 15), still moved the square. Let me check which square moved and in what direction.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check which square moved
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
magenta = [n for n in seg if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 4]
print("Magenta squares (top-left):")
for m in sorted(magenta, key=lambda x: (x['boundary'][0][0], x['boundary'][0][1])):
    print(f"  TL: ({m['boundary'][0][0]}, {m['boundary'][0][1]})")

# Previous positions were: (1,5), (29,33), (34,15), (55,46), (56,15)
# If (34,15) moved to (33,14), that's still (-1, -1)
# But I clicked (34, 17), not on the square itself
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Magenta squares (top-left):
  TL: (1, 5)
  TL: (29, 33)
  TL: (33, 16)
  TL: (55, 46)
  TL: (56, 15)

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Wait! The square at (34, 15) moved to (33, 16). That's (-1, +1), i.e., UP-RIGHT! Not up-left! So clicking to the RIGHT of the square makes it move up-right instead of up-left!
This changes everything. The direction depends on WHERE I click relative to the square:
- Clicking on the square (or to the left) → moves up-left (-1, -1)
- Clicking to the right → moves up-right (-1, +1)
Let me verify this. The square was at (34, 15) and I clicked (34, 17), which is to the right. The square moved to (33, 16), which is (-1, +1).
So the rule seems to be:
- Click on the square itself or to its left → moves up-left
- Click to the right of the square → moves up-right
This means I can control the horizontal direction by clicking left or right of the square! All squares always move UP, but the horizontal direction depends on where I click.
Now I can plan to move all squares to the blue blob! The blob is at rows 23-31, cols 29-37.
Current positions:
1. (29, 33) - already inside the blob
2. (33, 16) - needs to move up and RIGHT to reach the blob
3. (55, 46) - needs to move up and LEFT to reach the blob
4. (56, 15) - needs to move up and RIGHT to reach the blob
For square 2 at (33, 16):
- To reach row 29-31: needs 2-4 moves up
- To reach col 29-37: needs +13 to +21 columns right
- If I click to the right each time, it moves (-1, +1). After 13 moves: (20, 29), row too high.
- Hmm, I need a different approach. Maybe I can mix left and right clicks?
Actually, let me reconsider. Maybe the direction depends on which SIDE of the square I click:
- Click left side → moves left
- Click right side → moves right
- Always moves up
Let me test this by clicking to the LEFT of the square at (33, 16).

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Square at (33, 16) moved from (34, 15) after clicking (34, 17) - that's (-1, +1), up-right
# So clicking to the RIGHT of the square makes it move up-right
# Let me test clicking to the LEFT to see if it moves up-left
# Square at (33, 16) covers (33,16), (33,17), (34,16), (34,17)
# Click to the left: (33, 14)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 33, 'col': 14}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
